content.text: . React receives the already-computed result. On the first render this is fine, but on every subsequent render the expression is still evaluated — just so React can throw the value away. If the computation is cheap, this is negligible. If it parses a large JSON blob, reads from
, or does heavy filtering, it becomes an unnecessary bottleneck on every re-render.
, not a value. React calls the function and uses the return value; it does not call the function again on later renders.
is synchronous but still costs a DOM round-trip on every render. Wrapping it in a lazy initializer eliminates the overhead after mount.
When initial state is derived from a prop through an expensive transformation, the lazy form avoids recomputing on every re-render caused by the parent.

Building Initial State from URL or Config
There are two valid ways to write a lazy initializer:
The arrow function wrapper is more general: it works whether
takes arguments or not, and it clearly signals lazy intent. The bare function reference is a shorthand for zero-argument initializers.
The initializer function is a regular closure — it can close over any variable in scope, including props and external constants. It is called once with no arguments.
The initializer closes over props at mount time. If
later changes from the parent, the initializer does not re-run. Lazy initializers set state once — they are not reactive to prop changes.
: it should have no side effects and return the same value for the same inputs. React may call it in Strict Mode (development) an extra time to surface impurity.
Lazy Initializer vs useEffect for One-Time Setup
A common question: should one-time setup go in the lazy initializer or in a
No — must call setState, causing a second render
Parsing URL parameters or query strings at mount
Filtering, mapping, or sorting a large data set to produce the initial view
Creating a non-trivial data structure (e.g. building a lookup map from a flat array)
When You Do NOT Need Lazy Initialisation

Because the initializer runs only once, passing a different prop does not reset the state. The standard technique is the
unmounts and remounts it, running the initializer fresh.
You can test that the initializer runs only once by mocking the expensive function and counting calls.

In development, React Strict Mode intentionally calls the initializer function twice to expose impure code. Your initializer must produce the same value on both calls. If it does, Strict Mode discards the second result and uses the first. This is a test of correctness, not a runtime overhead.

Guard the initializer with a typeof window check:
Expecting the initializer to re-run when props change.
It won't. Use the key prop or a useEffect to reset state on prop changes.
Putting side effects in the initializer.
Network requests, subscriptions, or DOM mutations belong in useEffect, not in the initializer.
The eager form incurs the full computation cost every render; the lazy form pays it once at mount.
The same pattern applies to useReducer. Its third argument is an init function that receives the second argument and returns the initial state.
Multiple Lazy Initialisers in One Component
call is independent. You can mix lazy and eager forms in the same component depending on what each state variable needs.
